=== Rapid Response 2026: US-Israel war with Iran and ongoing impact – Vol. 4.031 Mar 2026 AutoIntelligence | Strategic Repor ===
On Feb. 28, 2026 (local time), the United States and Israel began an airstrike offensive against Iran, for numerous geopolitical reasons. The war continues as of March 30, 2026, and S&P Global Mobility has revised forecast assumptions. In 2026, Rapid Response continues to provide insights into global tariffs and other key industry activities that affect the industry and our vehicle production and sales forecasts. S&P Global Mobility’s fourth Rapid Response of 2026 discusses our latest forecast assumptions — and the potential for an oil price shock situation before the end of 2026. At the time of writing, the April 2026 light vehicle sales and production forecasts are in development. The figures here reflect our early draft thinking and are subject to some change. The light vehicle sales and production forecasts will be available to clients in mid-April; the next forecast round for the medium and heavy duty forecasts will be completed in May. As with our previous report, there are factors that could change our assessment, and we are not providing a probability as to whether the oil price shock scenario will or will not come to pass. If the conflict turns into a ground war, the outlook could change. The same is true if other countries get involved and to what degree. This assessment does not address broader potentials for ground war or further involvements. Strait of Hormuz restricted shipping disrupts global trade, with increasing impact as time goes on The Strait of Hormuz is a major thoroughfare for global trade; although it is not officially closed, the risk of a shipping tanker being caught in the crossfire and threats from the Iranian government have caused transport companies to avoid it and seek other routes. However, in recent days, oil tankers bound for India and China have been allowed to pass. Traffic disruption in the strait drastically affects economic activity and costs for any length of the war. GCC could see 2026 light vehicle sales lower by 200,000 units Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries have seen new vehicle supply disrupted. For countries reliant on sea imports, there will be vehicle shortages, longer wait times and reduced model availability. Inside the GCC, we expect rising vehicle prices and cost inflation. Freight, insurance and logistics costs will surge as goods are rerouted if possible and war-risk insurance premiums increase. Both factors drive up landed vehicle prices in the region and amplify GCC inflation across imported goods. Export constraints and cost inflation in the GCC will weaken fiscal conditions, dampen consumer sentiment and reduce credit appetite, which further softens demand for new vehicles and other imports. Against those conditions, the draft of our April 2026 total industry volume (TIV) light vehicle sales forecast sees the region at risk of losing about 200,000 units of vehicle sales. Global light vehicle sales could drop by 800,000 units in 2026 The global market will begin to feel a deeper impact as well if the strait remains closed through the month of April. Our March 2026 TIV base case assumptions were optimistic that tanker traffic would begin to flow after a couple of weeks and be restored by late March or early April. This has not happened. The base case assumptions behind our April 2026 TIV forecast now assume that the strait will remain essentially closed through April. Reopening the strait is expected to be a gradual process and normal shipping in the region would not resume until the second half of 2026. Presuming that the strait is reopened, confidence in the oil flows will rebuild as the situation stabilizes. Among the remaining uncertainties is that energy assets have been damaged during the war, but the extent of the damage and cost and time needed for repair is unknown. The S&P Global Mobility light vehicle April 2026 draft global sales forecast is seeing global light vehicle sales expectations reduced by 800,000-900,000 units and 500,000 units circa 2027; the 200,000-unit reduction in GCC sales is included in that figure. The impact on countries across the world is also likely to be uneven as well — we are not providing regional forecast yet. Initially, we see the current vehicle inventory state strong enough to absorb the impact of potential production cuts — the sales reduction is forecast based on the economic impact rather than lower production. The April 2026 draft reduction is in addition to a 500,000-unit impact the March 2026 global sales forecast reflected. It is also important to note that the figures reflect other macroeconomic impacts, further affordability pressure with the higher oil prices, and the direct impact to vehicle sales in the Middle East. Included in the April 2026 affordability impact assumptions is the expectation that higher energy inflation will result in central banks delaying interest rate cuts (versus raising rates). The economic issues will be led by increased oil prices. If the strait remains closed through April, S&P Global Energy (Energy) projects dated Brent to average US$120 per barrel in April, with daily swings ranging from US$100 to US$150. Within these assumptions, we see the market experiencing volatility and negative economic impact this year — but not a prolonged crisis. Light vehicle production impacts S&P Global Mobility analysts are also working on the next light vehicle production forecast. At this time, it is possible that the base case of the strait being closed through April might cause a larger reduction for 2026 than we have in the TIV sales side. It is not possible to determine guidance for the scope and severity of automaker-level impact. Relative to production, we see a host of known unknowns. It is evident that the Asia Pacific (APAC) region is most at risk and most impacted by the flow of oil and energy prices. Sourcing for the region, whether energy or goods, may require more significant changes and alternative supply patterns. The Middle East and Africa (MEA) are also larger end markets for ASEAN, Japan, South Korea and China production. Automakers in these regions will see more of the direct impact from lost MEA sales. Along with cost pressures, there are increasing concerns around feedstock coming from or through the region. These are also concerns that can affect the global industry but which APAC is also more attuned to for now. Concern over the supply of helium, needed for semiconductor manufacturing, so far is not affecting base case assumptions. Chip makers had roughly six months’ supply prior to the start of the war — at the same time, the longer the strait is closed, the more concern grows. Other potential supply concerns — and also known unknowns — include aluminum, methanol and bromine. Medium- and heavy-duty commercial industry impacts Some impacts to the medium- and heavy-duty commercial vehicle (MHCV) industry are similar to that of the light vehicle sector, including the potential for inflation and still-high (or rising) interest rates slowing consumer spending. However, the MHCV industry could see further impact from those factors also slowing business investment and trucking freight — along with economic impact for knock-on cost increases across the supply chain having an effect on goods transportation. The next MHCV forecast is due for May 2026; it is likely to see a further reduction than the March forecast, which reduced US 2026 MHCV demand by 1%. For the MHCV industry, high fuel costs at the pump have a more dramatic effect on total cost of ownership (TCO) and operation than for consumers. Our MHCV team sees potential for mix changes in the short term for the United States, specifically for buyers who substitute gasoline for diesel in medium trucks, particularly Class 4. The market may also see limited downsizing from large 15L diesel engines to smaller-displacement engines in Class 8 trucks. In markets outside of the US, particularly East Asia, the situation could create an elevated shift to EV solutions, although this is unlikely for the US under the current base case. Diesel is the more widely used fuel, and diesel costs go up along with gasoline costs. For the US, however, the MHCV team said that the revised base case for oil price assumptions does not increase cost enough to tip US TCO in favor of a zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) solution. For fleet operators considering such a change, they are also likely to face increased operating costs for the diesel trucks in their business while higher interest rates make borrowing more expensive and restrict available funds. Oil price shock scenario While this outcome will not be included in the April 2026 forecast assumptions, the Energy team sees the possibility for an oil price shock situation to arise. If the strait continues to be closed through May, there is potential for the Dated Brent price to reach US$200 per barrel in May 2026, although receding to US$170 per barrel in July as the strait opens and trade resumes. During that time, it is also possible to see extreme oil price swings on a daily basis. If Iran capitulates sooner, Energy sees potential for the price to come down quickly as well. If this scenario comes forward, S&P Global Mobility could reduce the 2026 sales forecast by between 4 million and 5 million units in 2026. This scenario could see the average oil price for 2026 reach US$129, which is 70% higher than our previous base case assessment. We would expect those conditions to create inflation double that of current expectations, with likely variability at a country level. Central banks will pause interest rate reductions and would be expected to begin to raise interest rates to cool inflation. These higher rates would drive higher mortgage rates and, although the impact on auto loans often lags, higher interest on auto loans. Higher oil prices at that level would sharply increase costs throughout the household: higher residential heating costs, higher goods costs related to higher transport costs, and higher goods costs related to more expensive plastics and other goods that incorporate oil. These higher costs lead to the modeled sharp decline in sales in 2026, with a significant impact in 2027 as well and lingering impact in 2028 and 2029. Forward look Our April 2026 forecast assumptions are aligned with S&P Global Market Intelligence, S&P Global Energy and S&P Global Economics and Country Risk teams, bringing a multidisciplinary view to the situation. Should the situation persist longer than we have presumed, there is risk for further economic harm, lower availability and higher cost of commodities and reduced manufacturing. As we previously noted, the duration of the war is the major factor affecting the severity of the damage and the effort needed to recover. Although the April 2026 forecast will incorporate a long disruption than the March forecast presumed, many expectations carry forward, but with greater impact. The impact of oil and natural gas pricing variables are disruptive owing to their cumulative effect. As commodity price increases take hold across the economy and supply chain, they will increasingly impact monthly household budgets and create a knock-on effect to lower vehicle sales.

=== CERAWeek 2026 Day Three: Top investing in energy takeaways25 Mar 2026 Energy Advisory Service - Energy View | Insight ===
Day 3 at CERAWeek here in Houston has wrapped up. Here are our key takeaways from the third day at the conference. Cautious optimism about Venezuela re-opening. Venezuela’s opposition leader and Nobel Peace Prize winner Maria Corina Machado was warmly welcomed by CERAWeek delegates, signaling optimism about the country’s new era. On energy, she laid out a vision that would welcome and encourage foreign investment to help rebuild the country’s oil and gas industry. Elsewhere across the conference, industry players outlined their near-term investment plans and expressed caution about the path forward. There was a consensus that the operating environment has improved and created some space for incremental investments. But big changes are still needed, industry executives said, to unlock the tens of billions of dollars in investment that will be needed for new large-scale projects, including improved investment terms, a clearer legal framework and more clarity on the future of the political situation. Mike Wirth, CEO of Chevron, the biggest US investor in Venezuela, said that his company was continuing incremental increases in output and investment but that his company was not yet ready to make large-scale spending decisions. He highlighted the need for better fiscal terms and access to international arbitration to resolve contract disputes among the required legal changes. Shell CEO Wael Sawan similarly said bigger changes were need but also said Shell could take FID on a project to export Venezuela-produced gas from nearby Trinidad and Tobago by the end of the year. ExxonMobil’s Dan Amman said the company had sent a team to Venezuela to evaluate investment opportunities. ConocoPhillips’ Ryan Lance, however, underscored the hurdles that still lay ahead, saying the company was still focused on recovering the roughly $12bn it won in international arbitration after its Venezuelan assets were nationalized. He also characterized recent changes to Venezuela’s hydrocarbon law as “woefully inadequate” and said investors would need to see that recent political changes are going to be durable before investing large amounts into the country again. International upstream exploration and investment prospects rebound on peaking shale and energy security focus. Among the biggest changes in tone from recent years at CERAWeek has been the return to optimism around international upstream exploration and investment. CERAWeek delegates pointed to two broadly supportive factors: more certainty around medium-term demand strength and expectations that US shale output was nearing its peak, which would increase the call on supply from other parts of the world. ConocoPhillips CEO Lance said that even with his company’s large shale business it would be investing to grow the exploration and conventional side of the company in the coming years, focused on Alaska and other regions. John Ardill, ExxonMobil’s VP for Global Exploration, said that globally the industry has been investing about half what it was in 2013 and spending on new exploration had become a hard sell internally at many companies, but the tone had shifted significantly. As the sector looks to a new era of exploration growth, the landscape and business model have changed, delegates said. Whereas a decade or so ago there was a bustling ecosystem of smaller explorers, today’s exploration is going to be led by larger companies. Those companies are likely to remain close to relatively proven oil and gas provinces, such as around Guyana and off West Africa, where there is less risk around geology and commercialization. As governments and companies put more emphasis on security and resilience, investing in international expansion across multiple regions will also help diversify supply and help mitigate against disruption risks, said Linda Cook, CEO of Harbour Energy. Companies are also turning to AI to help interpret seismic and other data to improve exploration success and to speed up and automate drilling and operational decision making. “Generational opportunity”: US gas industry dashes for data center demand growth. The huge buildout of data centers is creating a massive new opportunity for gas producers in the US both in on-grid power generation and to deliver gas directly to data centers for behind-the-meter generation. Pipeline developer TC Energy’s Tina Faraca said they saw the opportunity for 10bcf/d in demand growth from the power sector alone in the coming years and were investing to expand their network to help meet that demand. That would add significant new demand on top of booming LNG export growth, which could also see further expansions in the coming years in response to the crisis at the Strait of Hormuz. Dylan Seff, Head of Americas Utilities at trader Vitol, said they estimated two thirds of behind the meter data center generation would be gas as data centers prize around the clock generation and abundant gas supply. While this growth is seen as a big prize, industry players pointed to some significant hurdles to being able to fully capture the opportunity. Long wait times for large-scale gas turbines will crimp growth in on-grid gas generation through the rest of this decade, leaving growth dependent on squeezing more out of existing generators and behind the meter growth, Seff said. Permitting reform is also much needed to enable construction of new pipelines to deliver more gas to emerging data center demand hubs. Will Jordan of EQT said that the Appalachia region, which has been infrastructure constrained for years, could still grow significantly to meet LNG and data center demand with new pipelines. Interior Secretary Doug Burghum touted the administration’s efforts to get long stalled permitting reform passed, but it remains stuck in Congress. TC Energy’s Faraca said they were “seeing some momentum” on reform and she hoped the window of opportunity would yield reforms. Africa’s resource wealth moves into the spotlight in global competition to secure critical minerals and diversify fuel supplies. The current shock to oil and gas markets and global competition to secure critical minerals has opened huge new opportunities for companies to invest and develop new resource projects across Africa, which holds vast oil and gas reserves and roughly 30% of the world’s mineral wealth. But countries need to ensure the right policy frameworks are in place to enable long-term investment. Nikki Martin, President and CEO of EnerGeo Alliance, made clear that companies need to know that the rule of law and key fiscal or regulatory terms won’t change for 10-15 years to enable oil & gas development. Managing community relations is particularly important. More broadly, private sector investment will still require some degree of policy support to unlock mineral wealth for projects that are dependent on large capital outlays for infrastructure. Mining companies struggle with access to power, significantly increasing project cost and complexity. The US-China competition to secure mineral supply lines is unlocking capital for the region. The US Development Finance Corporation has committed $6 billion to develop the Lobito corridor, providing both transport infrastructure, and is now authorized to lend up to $205 billion towards these kinds of initiatives. Jon Hoffman, CEO of Globaleq, flagged that more money is needed for the corridor to invest into transmission, a big bottleneck for economic development across much of the continent. Countries across Africa will also see their own energy economies shift as resource development expands the economy, which will have global implications for energy markets. Overall, Africa’s energy future is an all-of-the-above approach. Countries like Nigeria with large fossil fuel reserves will use them for power to sustain industrial and economic development. But Microsoft Corporate VP Darryl Willis notes that there are many energy transitions across the continent and just as consumers leapfrogged landlines for mobile phones, many households will access cheaper more distributed clean power before more traditional continuous power generation systems using fossil fuels can be built out.

=== CERAWeek 2026 Day Two: Top investing in energy takeaways24 Mar 2026 Energy Advisory Service - Energy View | Insight ===
Day 2 at CERAWeek here in Houston has wrapped up. Here are our key takeaways from the second day at the conference: Iran crisis will push energy strategies and investment towards security and resilience. Delegates across CERAWeek this year pointed to the war with Iran as the latest in a series of major energy system shocks this decade that will push security and resilience to the top of the agenda for governments and companies. Shell CEO Wael Sawan argued that energy security would be paramount as the world tries to meet rising energy demand in the future, requiring an “all of the above approach” to energy investment while also pushing ahead with decarbonization. Security, however, will mean different things in different countries as they pursue their own domestic resources and economic strengths, with no “silver bullet” solution, said Sawan. This is evident in the diverging energy strategies between the US and China, as Beijing deploys its manufacturing prowess to accelerate electrification and the US leans into its vast oil and gas reserves. Countries with less clear-cut domestic options will pursue a diversity of technologies, fuels and sources of supply to mitigate against supply risks. For oil and gas markets there will be a need after the war with Iran to rebuild stockpiles, providing a tailwind for demand and likely keeping prices elevated over the medium term. Those stockpiles should be more highly valued than in the past as they provide continued resiliency. Energy companies will also need to rethink their own operations and supply chains with security at the top of the agenda. Olivier Le Peuch, CEO of SLB, said his company sought to localize its supply chains to the extent possible to maximize its operational flexibility. This pursuit of security is likely to result in an energy system that is more fragmented and less efficient. Renewed focus on security will likely buoy cleantech financing and valuations. The disruption at the Strait of Hormuz has exposed supply risks around major oil and gas trading chokepoints and will lead to an extended period of high commodity prices, which is likely to drive renewed interest in cleantech investment. The most immediate cleantech beneficiaries will be more mature renewable technologies that can be quickly deployed to address near term security and affordability challenges, including solar-plus-storage projects and standalone grid storage, said Amit Chandra head of Climate Technology at Barclays. Technologies that can help squeeze more power out of the existing grid and generation assets can also deliver quick wins on security and resilience, said Rhea Hamilton, Managing Director of General Atlantic. The crisis could also boost interest and help accelerate development of longer-term options such as traditional large-scale nuclear power developments, and more frontier advanced geothermal as well as small modular reactor nuclear technologies. These technologies are seen as promising over the long term to provide clean firm power to decarbonize the grid and provide resiliency in more renewable heavy grids. The AI buildout and strong interest from hyperscalers have already helped to push these technologies forward, but they still struggle with undeveloped supply chains, workforce shortages and struggles raising sufficient financing for early-stage projects. If governments step in to provide more support for these technologies as part of a broader energy security drive it could provide a needed backstop to encourage more private financing, said Chandra. Key to watch will be a couple of high profile SMR and geothermal initial public offerings tentatively planned for later this year, which will provide a barometer for financial markets’ appetite for frontier cleantech in this new environment. Energy companies are deploying AI widely in a bid to dramatically improve efficiency. Utilities, data center developers and oil and gas companies across CERAWeek are touting strong results as they deploy AI across their operations. Darryl Willis, Corporate Vice President of Energy & Resources Industry at Microsoft, said that AI-enabled predictive and prescriptive maintenance was leading to as much as a 50% reduction in unplanned shutdowns, improved safety, and minimized impacts on the environment. Page Crahan presented Alphabet’s Tapestry platform demonstrating the depth of disruption AI is having for utilities. Long interconnection queues, a major impediment to building out new generation and load, are on the cusp of being automated with agentic AI working through site assessments, comportment with routes and scoring, she said. AI is also helping to transform oil and gas operations. In upstream exploration, ExxonMobil’s John Ardill said that AI’s ability to process vast amounts of data was improving seismic data interpretation, allowing it to better see into and manage reservoirs. He also said AI was allowing greater automation across operations. A section of a recent offshore well in Guyana had been drilled fully autonomously, he said, relying on AI decision making. The company was looking to use AI to interpret its seismic data and autonomously identify new drilling prospects, which would be possible within months given how fast the technology is improving, he said. Western governments, miners, and investors have woken up to the challenge of competing with China’s cradle-to-grave approach to metals value chains, but building alternate supply lines will take time and capital . While the scale of capital needs to de-risk and diversify supply chains is manageable, getting investors to take greater risks in a sector with a long capex cycle and return profile will require policy interventions, said industry and financial players at CERAWeek. Western projects will struggle to compete with China on margins. As a result, an increasingly bifurcated pricing regime seems inevitable, but it’s unclear who among end-users has the appetite to accept a premium or structurally higher price to underwrite the costs of diversification and supply redundancy. Governments will have to find ways to de-risk investment, including at the sub-national level, using tax credits, price floors, price collars, tariffs, direct equity investments, and policy loans. From a capital markets perspective, the sector may benefit from growing investor interest as the market this year has seen a huge rotation of capital into asset-heavy, low obsolescence businesses like metals – a move that is likely to be reinforced by the Iran crisis. The US and other Western governments are unlikely to ever match the financial heft of Chinese industrial policy. But project developers will be able to turn to a robust venture capital ecosystem and deep capital markets capable of raising large sums of risk capital for tech development. Getting projects moving will require a crash effort from both the private and public sectors to develop a cohesive ecosystem for project development, including end-users who drive offtake agreements.
